metadata.paper_title: A Reference Architecture for Social Head Gaze Generation in Social Robotics

system_context.system_description: Architectures used for the generation of social head gaze in robots, mapping communicative and nonverbal functions to robot head gaze actions.
system_context.business_goals: To serve as a template for creating or re-engineering systems, provide analyses and understanding of different systems, and provide a common lexicon and taxonomy.

evaluation_setup.evaluation_goal: To evaluate, improve, and re-engineer existing head gaze system architectures, giving insight into their quality and determining if they meet requirements.

architectures.name: Human-Robot Collaboration Architecture
architectures.description: Supports engagement between a human and a humanoid robot by generating head gaze behaviors using specifications from human-human communication for conversational turn taking and engagement.

key_components.responsibilities: Perception (turn status), dialog content, and behavior arbitration (inhibiting turn/pointing gestures).
Generates head gaze required for engaging in a conversation.
Generates head gaze necessary for looking interested in humans.
Generates referential head gazes for looking at objects in the environment.

assumptions: Validated for a humanoid robot 'Melvin' in a tangram game task.

architectures.name: Robot Behavior Toolkit Architecture
architectures.description: Provides a framework for generating head gaze in human-like robots using specifications for conversational turn taking and referential gaze.

key_components.responsibilities: Collection of behavioral specifications in XML.
Queries the Knowledge Base for an appropriate behavior based on the percept.
Resolves conflicts and overlaps among behaviors by prioritization.
Provides internal and external percepts based on information from the Perceptual System and current action.

assumptions: Validated for a simulated PR2 and a physical humanoid robot 'Wakamaru'.

scenarios.scenario_text: Overall functionality: Evaluate the number of head gaze behaviors supported by the architecture.
Adaptation to a new environment: Factors that can change are the robot (e.g. search and rescue) and dialog.
Extension of capability: Extension of the architectures to a multi-party scenario (requiring addition of new behaviors and production rules).

scenario_classification.rationale: Requires modification of subsystems to support new environments.
Requires addition of new behaviors and rules.

issues_identified: Does not generate head gaze for 'Show various mental states'.
Does not generate head gaze for 'Convey general liveliness and awareness'.
Does not generate head gaze for 'Show various mental states'.
Does not generate head gaze for 'Convey general liveliness and awareness'.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Upgrade perception, dialog management, and arbitration for new environment.
issues_identified: Collaboration Manager is monolithic; limited structural separation between perception, dialog, and arbitration.
Coordination mechanisms exist in both Collaboration Manager and Maintenance Policy and are not fully isolated.
Serial flow of data between Turn Policy and Reference Policy affects real-time performance.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Adaptation to different robots and dialog.
issues_identified: None significant; offers adequate support as subsystems are isolated and non-monolithic.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Addition of new behaviors for multi-party scenario.
issues_identified: Collaboration Manager functionalities need upgrade and are monolithic.
Use of the same subsystem for different behaviors reduces maintainability and reusability.
Subsystems are solely developed for a rule-based system; complex extensions like learning are difficult.

architectural_strengths: RBT offers adequate support for re-engineering to new environments due to isolated, non-monolithic subsystems.
HRC components represent individual social head gaze functions well.
architectural_weaknesses: HRC has a monolithic Collaboration Manager with poor separation of concerns.
HRC has serial data flow between Turn and Reference policies.
RBT has a monolithic Knowledge Base.
RBT is limited to rule-based systems.
Both architectures lack nonverbal functions (Projecting Mental State, Establishing Agency).

architecture_comparison.rationale: Better support for adaptation to new environments due to isolated subsystems, though extension of capability is hindered by monolithic Knowledge Base.
Inadequate support for adaptation due to monolithic Collaboration Manager and serial dependencies affecting performance.

recommendations: For HRC: Isolate perception and coordination functions from the Collaboration Manager.
For HRC: Make Turn Policy independent of Reference Policy to improve real-time performance.
For RBT: Subdivide Knowledge Base into independent self-contained components.
For RBT: Upgrade subsystems to support advanced techniques like learning, not just rules.

inference_notes: Inferred 'Indirect' classification for S-02 and S-03 as the text describes necessary architectural changes or improvements.
S-01 is treated as a functionality assessment; issues identified were missing features.
